translations.translation:
Zhu Ju (Zifan)
朱據 (子範)
Lived AD 194–250
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]

byZL181
onJanuary 22nd, 2024

Zhu Ju, styledZifan, was fromWu[吳],Wu commandery[吳郡].
He was handsome and strong, and also adept in debating difficult topics.
In the beginning of theera, he was summoned to bePalace Gentleman for All Purposesand then filled the post ofImperial Clerk. At the time, theSecretariat Minister of the Nomination Bureau,Ji Yan, detested the corruption and filth in offices, and wanted to purify and cleanse them.[Zhu] Jubelieved that, “The realm is not yet stable. You ought to let them render achievements for them to vanquish their faults, and to abandon their shortcomings and appoint them instead. Nominating the pure and censuring the corrupt would be enough to stop [wicked deeds] and promote [virtuous deeds]. If you were to demote or expel them at a single moment, I fear there would be future grievances.”[Ji] Yan, who did not listen, ultimately failed.
[Sun] Quanwould sigh loudly over his [deceased] generals, venting sadness and sighing, and rememberingLü MengandZhang Wen. He believed that[Zhu] Ju, who had talent in both civil and military affairs, could succeed them. Hence, he conferred him asColonel who Establishes Righteousnessand had him lead troops to garrisonHushu[湖孰].
In the first year of theera [229],[Sun] Quanmoved the capital toJianye[建業].[Zhu] Juwas summoned and he married aprincess. He was conferred asGeneral of the Leftand enfeoffed asMarquisofYunyang[雲陽侯]. He was modest and humble, and he received scholars. He viewed wealth as trivial and enjoyed giving it out, so though his salary and gifts were enormous, he would often not have enough wealth.
During theera, Great Cash coins were first cast. One of them is equal to five hundred. Later, the personal troops of[Zhu] Juwere to receive thirty thousand stringed coins, but an artisan,Wang Sui, swindled and received them.ExaminerLü Yisuspected that[Zhu] Juactually seized them and harshly interrogated the overseer,1who died under the beating stick.[Zhu] Jumourned him for his innocence and used a spacious coffin to coffin him.[Lü] Yithen memorialized, “The subordinate official of[Zhu] Ju2concealed [the embezzlement] for[Zhu] Ju, who thus accorded him a generous encoffinment.”[Sun] Quaninterrogated[Zhu] Jumultiple times, but[Zhu] Juhad nothing with which to explain himself, so he sat on a grass mat and awaited his punishment. After several months, an official of the Organized Army,Liu Zhurevealed and said thatWang Suiwas the one who seized [the money].[Sun] Quanthus had a great revelation and said, “If evenZhu Jureceived injustice, what more is there to say of the officials and commonfolk as well?” He then extensively investigated the crimes of[Lü] Yiand awarded[Liu] Zhuonemillion.•••
The “overseer” [主者] was presumably the person in charge of handing out the stringed coins.↩
It is unknown if the “subordinate official of[Zhu] Ju” [據吏] is the same person as the “overseer” [主者] or if they are two different people, as the “overseer” is never explicitly described as being under the employ ofZhu Ju.Lü Yiseems to suggest that they are to explain why the “overseer” was given a spacious coffin, but he could be lying.↩
In the ninth year of theera [246], he was promoted toGeneral of Coursers. When the two palaces3bickered and contended,[Zhu] Jufervently supported theHeir Apparent, and whenever he spoke, it was with extreme sincerity and righteousness was on his expression. He would adhere to such even with death.AHe was demoted toDeputyofXindu commandery[新都郡丞]. He had not arrived when theDirector of the Central Depository,Sun Hong, slandered[Zhu] Juand took advantage of the bedridden illness of[Sun] Quan.[Sun] Hongthen created an imperial edict, which forced his suicide. He was fifty-seven at the time.
During the era ofSun Liang, his two sons,[Zhu] Xiongand[Zhu] Sun, also each led troops,4but they were slandered byPrincess Quan. They were all killed. During theera, those with merits in the past were remembered, and so the son of[Zhu] Xiong,[Zhu] Xuaninherited the peerage to becomeMarquisofYunyang[雲陽侯] and married a princess.
During the time ofSun Hao,[Zhu] XuanbecameGeneral of Coursers.•••
Refers to the princesSun HeandSun Ba, who had their own palaces.↩
These were presumably troops that were formerly under the command of their father,Zhu Ju.↩
TheCoherent SayingsbyYin Jicontains the argument of[Zhu] Ju, which states: “I have heard that theHeir Apparentis the base and roots of the empire. He is benevolent and filial by nature, and the world wholeheartedly submits to him. Yet now that you have suddenly condemned him, this will lead to him being stressed out quickly. Of old, when theAstute[Duke] of Jin [晉獻公] used Concubine Li [驪姬],Shenshengdid not survive;5when theMartial[Emperor] ofHantrusted Jiang Chong [江充], the UnrepentantHeir Apparent[戾太子] was unjustly killed.6I humbly fear that theHeir Apparentcannot handle such stress,7and though you may erect a palace grieving your son, nothing can be revertedback.”•••
Under the urging of Concubine Li to place her own son, Xiqi, as the heir,Shenshengwas killed. Jin fell into turmoil soon after.↩
Emperor Liu Che believed false claims from Jiang Chong that his son, Liu Ju, was plotting rebellion. Liu Ju, after being forced to rebel for his life, committed suicide when he was surrounded. Liu Che built the Palace which Grieves the Son to show his remorse.↩
The implication is thatSun Hewould commit suicide.↩
